records.ety: [OE. bluschen to shine, look, turn red, AS. blyscan to glow; akin to blysa a torch, āblsian to blush, D. blozen, Dan. blusse to blaze, blush.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Blushed (blsht); p. pr. & vb. n. Blushing.]

defs: To become suffused with red in the cheeks, as from a sense of shame, modesty, or confusion; to become red from such cause, as the cheeks or face.
quotes: To the nuptial bower I led her blushing like the morn.
In the presence of the shameless and unblushing, the young offender is ashamed to blush.
He would stroke The head of modest and ingenuous worth, That blushed at its own praise.

defs: To grow red; to have a red or rosy color.
quotes: The sun of heaven, methought, was loth to set, But stayed, and made the western welkin blush.

defs: To have a warm and delicate color, as some roses and other flowers.
quotes: Full many a flower is born to blush unseen.

defs: To suffuse with a blush; to redden; to make roseate.
quotes: To blush and beautify the cheek again.

defs: To express or make known by blushing.
A suffusion of the cheeks or face with red, as from a sense of shame, confusion, or modesty.
A red or reddish color; a rosy tint.
quotes: Light's last blushes tinged the distant hills.
notes: This phrase is used now more of ideas, opinions, etc., than of material things. “All purely identical propositions, obviously, and at first blush, appear,” etc. Locke.
